mappings.rationaleAI: Singapore's Four Asian Tigers economic transformation and world's highest PPP GDP per capita exemplify economic growth in developing countries (A.4), while its government-directed industrialization connects to economic planning in developing countries (B.4).

Poverty's relationship to per capita income and population growth directly addresses the link between underdevelopment and low GDP per capita (A.4.a), while reduction strategies and social costs connect to social costs and benefits of economic growth (A.3) and economic growth in developing countries (A.4).

Economics as a discipline encompasses macroeconomic growth theory and models (A.2), the nature and causes of economic growth (A), and fiscal and monetary policy tools relevant to planning for economic growth and stability (B).

Smith's Wealth of Nations addresses division of labour, capital accumulation, and free markets as factors influencing economic growth (A.1), while his foundational economic theory contributes to the theory of economic growth (A.2).

Macroeconomics directly addresses GDP, growth theory and models (A.2), economic productivity and output measurement (A.5), and the role of monetary and fiscal policy in planning for economic stability (B).

Marx's historical materialism, critique of capitalism, and surplus value theory contribute to theories of national economic development (A.2), while his vision of communism and planned economies directly relates to economic planning in Communist countries (B.2).

Taxation as governmental fiscal policy is a factor influencing economic growth (A.1), and tax policy as a tool of economic regulation connects to economic planning in developed non-Communist countries (B.3).

Capitalism's mechanisms of capital accumulation, market competition, and supply-and-demand equilibrium address factors influencing economic growth (A.1) and theories of growth based on market-driven resource allocation (A.2).

The economy as the systemic domain of production, distribution, and consumption provides the foundational context for understanding the nature of economic growth (A) and the role of planning for economic stability (B).

Ibn Khaldun's proto-macroeconomics including supply-demand analysis, labor theory of value, and cyclical dynasty theory contribute to theories of economic growth (A.2) and theories of national economic development and retardation (A.4.b).
Socialism's planned, state-directed, and self-managed economic models directly address the nature of economic planning (B.1) and economic planning in Communist countries (B.2).

Technology as a driver of productivity and economic transformation is a primary factor influencing economic growth (A.1), and its impact on output per unit of input directly relates to economic productivity (A.5).
Markets and trade networks, from comparative advantage to globalization, function as a factor influencing economic growth through specialization and resource allocation (A.1).

Malaysia's transformation to an upper-middle-income economy with strengths in electronics and petrochemicals exemplifies economic growth in developing countries (A.4) and economic planning in developing countries (B.4).

Bangladesh's garment-export-driven economy in a densely populated, climate-vulnerable developing country illustrates the relationship between underdevelopment and per capita income relative to population growth (A.4.a).

Work as a factor of production alongside capital addresses the labour force as an influence on economic growth (A.1), while the division of labor and automation debates connect to changes in economic productivity (A.5).

Poland's post-war communist rule under Soviet influence connects to economic planning in Communist countries (B.2), while its rapid post-1989 democratic transition and economic growth illustrate planning in developed non-Communist countries (B.3) and growth dynamics (A.4).
